# Understanding the scaling up of future innovations with techno-economic analysis

[*Nature Reviews Clean Technology*](/nrct)
**volume 1**, page 232 (2025)[Cite this article](#citeas)

136 Accesses

1 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/s44359-025-00046-0/metrics)

### Subjects

The scale-up and translation of lab-scale inventions into the market requires an understanding of technical and economic performance of a new product or technology. This information is needed to understand potential market competitiveness, make decisions about product and process design, and speed up commercialization. Techno-economic analysis (TEA) assesses how well innovations function by evaluating both technical and economic performance through the development of cost models and process modelling.

Sustainability assessments are incomplete without TEAs, as they weigh a product’s benefits against its costs from cradle to gate. They are also an important part of scaling up early-stage innovations as they ease the decision-making process when determining whether these innovations are ready for the market. For example, TEAs are being used to understand techno-economic aspects of using advanced green solvents such as ionic liquids in a variety of applications from CO2 capture and utilization to bio-based textiles. Process modelling with sensitivity and uncertainty analysis steps can help to optimize the energy use within the system, track the carbon emissions, identify the basic cost indicators and process scaling-up risks.

Techno-Economic Analysis: A Tool for Lean Startups
==================================================

[Tools of the Trade](https://forgeimpact.org/category/tools-of-the-trade/)

![Image 3: Next Rung Technology logo: Techno-Economic Analysis: A Tool for Lean Start-Ups](https://forgeimpact.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Next-Rung-Technology-logo.png)

The team of experienced engineers at [Next Rung Technology](https://nextrungtechnology.com/), [a Friend of FORGE partner](https://forgeimpact.org/become-a-partner/), collaborates with growth-stage companies creating sustainable technologies to support them through the range of complex challenges faced in commercialization. In this Tools of the Trade, Next Rung Technology goes in-depth on how lean startups can use techno-economic modeling to position themselves for success.

* * *

In today’s fiercely competitive clean-tech industry, the concept of “lean” has emerged as a powerful methodology for start-ups. Discover the power of techno-economic modeling as a tool for companies to ask critical questions and gain strategic insights, paving the way to rapid commercialization and positioning them for success.

The concept of “lean” has risen to the forefront these days, as a way to denote projects that have optimized their production processes and increased profitability. The idea behind lean projects is to identify and eliminate waste from a process, thereby reducing costs and maximizing productivity. Waste in this context can be thought of not only in terms of physical streams but also in terms of inefficient allocation of effort and capital. By adopting a lean approach, companies can cut unnecessary expenditures and redirect resources to higher-value activities.

### **The Lean Clean-Tech Start-Up**

Start-ups have embraced lean practices as a means to accelerate success by adopting tools to shorten development cycles and minimize wasted resources. To test hypotheses and support agile technology development, techno-economic analyses (TEAs) can serve as a critical point in the feedback loop, providing companies with a means to rapidly focus research efforts, obtain real results, and facilitate iterative design. An effective TEA can support several lean methods:

![Image 4: Image 1. Lean start-up methods that can be supported by techno-economic modeling.](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63692ed6c3a0e706ed03fc3d/439ad8dd-856f-43c2-a011-9b83af20fc86/Screen+Shot+2023-05-25+at+10.03.45+AM.png)

Image 1. Lean start-up methods that can be supported by techno-economic modeling.

These methods are crucial for launching projects quickly and inexpensively while reducing risk. Despite their evident value, TEAs present many challenges to clean-tech start-ups. Accurately simulating a process at pilot or commercial scale often proves to be a difficult task, requiring involvement of outside consultants with expertise in plant design and operation. Next Rung Technology has used our industry experience to develop countless TEAs that have become invaluable tools to our clients as they strive to scale their operations.

### **The Next Rung Technology Approach to TEA**

The Next Rung team takes a collaborative and forward-thinking approach when conducting techno-economic analyses, working closely with clients to tailor each TEA model to their unique processes and development interests.

![Image 5: Image 2. The Next Rung TEA process from project definition to a ready-to-use model.](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63692ed6c3a0e706ed03fc3d/08b256f2-a6a5-4e2e-96d1-ba2503a12292/Screen+Shot+2023-05-25+at+10.04.49+AM.png)

Image 2. The Next Rung TEA process from project definition to a ready-to-use model.

![Image 6: Image 3. A standard Next Rung TEA faceplate.](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63692ed6c3a0e706ed03fc3d/7bb449b5-0882-4175-8e8f-0225f7f20f85/Screen+Shot+2023-05-25+at+10.21.34+AM.png)

Image 3. A standard Next Rung TEA faceplate.

While techno-economic models can become overwhelming in their depth and complexity, Next Rung prioritizes simplicity over marginal gains in accuracy. Our aim is to create an iterative and workable tool for clients to actively use throughout the development of their technology, rather than providing a single-point third-party assessment of economics. As a smaller firm, NRT is able to stay highly dynamic in our approach— regular feedback from the client to ensures the development of the model meets their needs, which may change as the process unfolds.

The final model features a user-friendly faceplate that enables scenario modeling, highlights key outcomes such as production capacity or environmental metrics, and provides a detailed breakdown of economics including cost of goods sold (COGS), operating expenses (OPEX), and capital expenses (CAPEX). Our comprehensive model is summarized in a clear and concise display of essential metrics that are vital to companies seeking funding, aiming to minimize costs, or deciding where to direct funding for technology development.

By leveraging the power of TEA and adopting a lean mindset, the following companies described below were able to reduce costs and position themselves for sustainable growth.

### **Case Study #1: Helping a Client Prioritize R&D for a Lab-Scale Process**

n early-stage biopharmaceutical company wanted to develop a TEA to understand the economics of their existing bench-scale process at the commercial scale. A goal of the process was to narrow down the company’s broad search for process drivers that could improve performance and optimize production while reducing overall costs.

The results of the TEA identified which parameters had the greatest impact, providing guidance on where to focus their research efforts. This project helped the company avoid one of the most common pitfalls for start-ups— chasing down process improvements with minimal potential for economic gains— to avoid wasted R&D efforts and operate in a lean manner.

### **Case Study #2: Eliminating Uncertainty Around a Client’s Downstream Process**

![Image 7: Image 4. Identifying most influential drivers to focus R&D efforts during product development](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63692ed6c3a0e706ed03fc3d/1edcc310-685e-4e42-ab64-37b5200ed960/Screen+Shot+2023-05-25+at+10.46.10+AM.png)

Image 4. Identifying most influential drivers to focus R&D efforts during product development.

After working with Next Rung to build their initial TEA model, a later-stage food ingredient start-up sought to screen eight downstream processing options being considered for their product. Rather than dedicating company resources toward extensive laboratory testing, the client utilized the modeling tool for initial evaluations of their options.

Next Rung built multiple parallel models to compare final product purity, overall recovery, and other critical process outcomes. The models identified the downstream options with higher projected OPEX, perhaps due to increased labor or utility requirements, versus cases that would likely require more expensive equipment, thus incurring a larger capital investment. By uncovering hidden expenses and inefficiencies, the client was able to take the lean approach and reduce their options to the most promising processes. Through the TEA process, the client achieved a better understanding of their costs and a refined focus for process design efforts before scaling up commercially.

For us, a TEA is not just a cost estimation exercise. It is also an opportunity to critically assess clients’ process design parameters and performance, leading to better outcomes overall. In building a TEA, Next Rung values the process as much as the final product— keeping our clients as active collaborators and maintaining a flexible and iterative model is key to using TEA as a _lean_ tool. If you are curious if our TEA services are right for you, [please don’t hesitate to reach out](https://www.nextrungtechnology.com/contact) , we would love to discuss what you are working on!

Techno-economic assessment (TEA) is a useful tool to estimate economic performance, and identify bottlenecks for the development of biofuel and chemical production technology, meanwhile, life cycle assessment (LCA) is applied to assess sustainability by reducing the environmental impact of biofuel and chemical production. Highlights
----------

*   •Review TEA research progress in the manufacturing of biofuels. 
*   •Review LCA research progress in the manufacturing of biofuels. 
*   •Enable viable and sustainable biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals. 
*   •Guidance of TEA and LCA in the manufacturing of biofuels and chemicals. 
*   •Challenges of TEA and LCA in the manufacturing of biofuels and chemicals. 

Abstract
--------

To reduce the dependency on petroleum-based products and emission of greenhouse gas, renewable biofuels and chemicals play an important role to meet the unmatched energy demands of the rapidly growing population. However, most biofuel and chemical products do not reach the commercialization stage, mainly hindered by incomparable economics to petroproducts. Techno-economic assessment (TEA) is a useful tool to estimate economic performance, and identify bottlenecks for the development of biofuel and chemical production technology, meanwhile, [life cycle assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/life-cycle-engineering "Learn more about life cycle assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LCA) is applied to assess sustainability by reducing the environmental impact of biofuel and chemical production. This present review covers TEA and LCA research progress in the manufacturing of biofuels and biochemical, and discusses the impacts of TEA and LCA results on the development and optimization of biofuel and chemical production. In addition, challenges associated with TEA and LCA of biofuel and biochemical production were briefly overviewed, and potential approaches that may overcome such challenges were discussed enabling viable and sustainable biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals. Future integrated TEA and LCA studies could significantly promote the economic and sustainable development of the biomanufacturing process.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fossil-fuel "Learn more about Fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), occupying 70%–80% world's fuel energy, are increasingly demanded and associated with global [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the rapid grown world population. Based on the geologic resources supply-demand model, global fossil fuel demand will continuously grow till 2025 or prolong to 2075 in different scenarios, then decline [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib1)], leading to fossil fuel exhaustion possibly in less than 50 years [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib2)]. Besides, fossil fuel and related petroproduct production has posed a detrimental irreversible effect on the environment, exacerbating global warming due to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. To meet these challenges reducing the dependence on fossil fuels, [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as biofuels and bio-based chemicals attract great attention as a substitute for traditional fossil fuels and petroproducts.

As shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#tbl1), the fuels and chemicals derived from bio-based routes contribute less GHG emissions compared with those from petroleum-based routes. [Ethanol production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ethanol-production "Learn more about Ethanol production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the USA has more than tripled from 2.8 billion gallons/year up to 9 billion gallons/year. As the second largest [bioethanol](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioethanol "Learn more about bioethanol from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") producer, Brazil provides approximately 5 billion gallons/year [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib3)]. [Biodiesel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biodiesel "Learn more about Biodiesel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") production in the USA lower than bioethanol is also growing rapidly, increasing over tenfold from 2004 to 2008 [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib4)]. By 2030, over 38 billion gallons per year of renewable biofuels will be produced and consumed in the USA [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib5)]. Currently, biofuels and chemicals have been produced through biomanufacturing from various [feedstocks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/feedstock "Learn more about feedstocks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

The first-generation biofuel platform uses food crops like corn, cane, starch, and vegetable oil as feedstock, causing great pressure on the price and availability of food, called a ‘‘food versus fuel” conflict [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib6)]. Lignocellulosic-based biofuel known as the second-generation biofuels may be a promising alternative, limited by low fermentable sugar content and costly biomass [pretreatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pretreatment "Learn more about pretreatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process. Researchers exploded liquid biofuels from [microalgae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microalga "Learn more about microalgae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") biomass, called the third-generation biofuels, which can be upgraded into several types of renewable and eco-friendly fuels (jet fuel, petrol, and diesel). However, most production processes of biofuels and chemicals, especially the second and third-generation biofuels and chemicals do not enter the commercialization stage to date, mainly due to their relatively lower profitability and higher selling price compared to petroproducts.

The economics of biofuel and chemical production can be efficiently estimated by using techno-economic assessment (TEA). TEA is an important assessment tool for investigating economic metrics like capital and operation costs based on modeling and simulation of technologies. TEA simultaneously analyzes both the technical and economic performance of a product or a process [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib7)], which has been used for technology development and commercialization in a wide range of different industries.

TEA can accomplish many tasks, including investigation of cash flows over a lifetime, evaluation of potential economic feasibility of a specific technology or process, evaluation of different technology applications likelihood, and economic comparison of different techniques for producing the same products. For the biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals, TEA results can help researchers to analyze the major elements of capital and operation costs and quantify the minimum selling price. By identifying sensitive influence factors on the economics of biofuel and chemicals, further development and optimization of the production process can be conducted to reduce selling prices compared to their functional equivalent petroproducts. TEA enables the development and optimization of biomanufacturing from technological and economic perspectives, however environmental impacts are not involved, which play a key role in sustainable technology and process development.

Given the overall life cycle of the biomanufacturing process, [life cycle assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/life-cycle-engineering "Learn more about life cycle assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LCA) mainly focuses on the environmental impact by considering all energy and resource inputs and multiple environmental emission outputs, instead of technical and economic feasibility and economic profitability [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib7)]. Typically, LCA quantifies the mass and energy exchanges between the processing system and the entire environment with “cradle-to-grave” system boundaries that include biomass pretreatment, biosynthesis pathway, production stage, and end-of-life handlings such as waste treatment for biofuel and chemicals. In contrast, TEA for biofuel and chemicals is generally limited in the “cradle-to-gate” system boundaries of that production stage excluding the use stage and waste treatment. To assess the biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals from both economic and environmental aspects, TEA and LCA can be combined providing more information needed for further sustainable process development. The integration of TEA and LCA can simultaneously evaluate the technical feasibility, economic performance, and environmental impacts, providing a systematic and comprehensive analysis of the relationships between environment, technology, and economy, as well as a trade-off analysis for the biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals.

This paper reviews recent TEA and LCA publications in the open literature for biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals, focusing on methodologies of TEA and LCA, TEA and LCA studies conducted for producing different types of fuels and chemicals, particularly using the second and third generation biomass. Challenges associated with TEA and LCA are also presented for the development of economic and eco-friendly biomanufacturing of chemicals and fuels.

2. TEA for biomanufacturing
---------------------------

### 2.1. Definition of TEA

TEA is a methodology framework, that includes process simulation and engineering design with economic analysis, to analyze the potential technological and economic feasibility of a product or process [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib23)]. TEA is a powerful tool to address many issues during technology development, including cash flow investigation over a lifetime, cost and profitability estimation, economic sensitive factors identification, and quantitatively economic comparison of different technologies. Besides evaluating the economic benefits of technologies with high technology readiness levels (TRLs) of 8 or 9, TEA can help to identify and eliminate bottlenecks of technologies with low TRLs of 2–3, guiding the subsequent scale-up and commercial development process [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib24)]. Therefore, TEA has been widely used by academic and industrial researchers to evaluate and optimize the new technologies or processes in bio-manufacturing systems of various [bioproducts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioproducts "Learn more about bioproducts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib23)].

TEA typically included five steps: process design, mass and energy balance, cost estimations, profitability analysis, and sensitivity analysis ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#fig1)). Starting from the general process flow diagram, a process simulation can be developed by different software to calculate the material and energy balances of each operation unit in the integrated process. Based on the mass and energy balance results, a process design including equipment requirements of each operation unit was conducted to assist next cost estimation. Once total capital investment (TCI) and annual operating cost (AOC) were determined, a discounted cash flow analysis over an established lifetime was used to estimate commonly economic metrics of the overall process, such as minimum selling price (MSP), net present value (NPV), and internal rate of return (IRR).

Fig.1. The framework of TEA. TEA for the biomanufacturing process starts with the collection of raw data, followed by various assessment steps to estimate economic metrics that could further guide the biomanufacturing process development.

MSP, the price needed to reach an NPV of zero with a set IRR of 10%, is one of the most important metrics in published TEAs of biorefinery to evaluate the market competitiveness of biomanufacturing fuels and chemicals [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib25)]. The economic performance established here inherently carried relatively high uncertainty which can be subsequently evaluated by sensitivity analysis and risk assessment. Significant impact factors on economic metrics of biomanufacturing processes can be determined by sensitivity analysis, and results could guide future process optimization and improvement by demonstrating the possible strategy to eliminate the bottlenecks and achieve better economic feasibility of biomanufacturing fuels and chemicals.

### 2.2. Application and progress of TEA in biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals

For the biomanufacturing process, TEA is often adopted to evaluate economic performance and estimated economic feasibility, identify sensitive factors of economic metrics, and provide guidance for future improvement on both technical and economic aspects. Many research studies of TEA in the biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals have been conducted to explore the potential economic profitability and comparability of bio-based fuels and chemicals, compared with traditional [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and petrochemicals. Different [feedstock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/feedstock "Learn more about feedstock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and/or the different production pathways are also compared for process development and optimization. The main bio-based products of interest in the current studies include [bioethanol](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioethanol "Learn more about bioethanol from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), biofuel, and value-added chemicals such as lactic acid, [succinic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/succinic-acid "Learn more about succinic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), acrylic acid, [butanol](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/butanol "Learn more about butanol from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and polyhydroxy butyrate (PHB). In this section, the TEA studies related to the biomanufacturing of fuels and chemicals are reviewed by product type.

#### 2.2.1. Bioethanol

Bioethanol is converted from lignocellulosic biomass, (_e.g._[corn stover](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/corn-stover "Learn more about corn stover from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [switchgrass](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/panicum-virgatum "Learn more about switchgrass from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and hardwood), usually _via_ dilute-acid [pretreatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pretreatment "Learn more about pretreatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [enzymatic hydrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/enzymatic-hydrolysis "Learn more about enzymatic hydrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and microorganism fermentation [[[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib28)]. Kazi et al. [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666952822000802#bib29)] compared the impact of four pretreatment technologies and three downstream process parameters on the commercial viability of cellulosic [ethanol production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ethanol-production "Learn more about ethanol production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from corn stover. The dilute-acid pretreatment gives the lowest ethanol production cost of 5.13 USD/gal of gasoline-equivalent (gge), which is 1.36 USD/L of liter of gasoline-equivalent (lge) due to the higher sugar and ethanol yield. The MSP value of cellulosic ethanol varied in a range of 0.8–4.58 USD/gal due primarily to different assumptions in feedstock cost, process assumptions, and co-products, et al. The most sensitive uncertainty factor is the cost of [cellulase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cellulase "Learn more about cellulase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") enzymes and the feedstock price, which contributed to a high degree of uncertainty about the economic feasibility of cellulosic ethanol production.

# A guide to techno-economic analysis for early-stage founders

![Writer: Max Werny]()

Updated: Apr 22

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_fa0c7681e494437bb83edff3525d340d~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_28,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_fa0c7681e494437bb83edff3525d340d~mv2.png)

There’s nothing like a good TEA. And no, this (unfortunately) does not have anything to do with Earl Grey, Darjeeling or matcha tea. The TEA we’re talking about stands for **techno-economic analysis** and is all about **understanding your business** and building for success!

Given its importance for every hard tech startup, we decided to put together a short, concise TEA guide for early-stage founders. A TEA can seem daunting at first but very often turns out to be an incredibly useful tool for founders. Our mission is to make this journey slightly less bumpy and therefore more enjoyable for entrepreneurs. Read on to find out more!

## **Why do a TEA?**

A techno-economic analysis (TEA) is an evaluation tool that **combines technical and economic aspects to assess the feasibility, competitiveness, and potential impact of a technology or project**. It can provide you with a detailed understanding of your unit economics, cost drivers, technical key performance indicators (KPIs), as well as the benefits and risks associated with the implementation of your technology.

**You can use a TEA to drive your strategy and decision-making**: by understanding the most important metrics influencing the unit economics and commercial viability, you can start to build a roadmap to bring down cost drivers or optimise important performance parameters.

As every TEA is different, we recommend founders to use multiple resources to construct a TEA that fits their process or product (more on this at the end of the article). TEAs for pre-seed and seed companies will naturally differ from TEAs for later-stage companies as there are more unknown parameters and thus greater uncertainty. But don’t let this deter you - **a TEA is a tool that comes in handy even at the earliest stages of company building**!

Really, a TEA is living document that evolves with your company, and one that grows in fidelity as you get greater confidence in the input parameters. Most crucially, remember that it isn’t simply a tick-box exercise for investors to show you have one in your data room.

## **What goes into a TEA?**

Like a good cuppa tea, you need to get the ingredients right - see our graphic below for the most essential ingredients:

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_89ed8390f6364fdcb9645c5683dd5907~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_28,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_89ed8390f6364fdcb9645c5683dd5907~mv2.png)

*Relevant information that should go into a techno-economic analysis (list is not exhaustive).*

For those of you who want to get to the bottom of this, take a look at our TEA checklist below (list is not exhaustive):

A **schematic figure** (e.g. process flow diagram), especially if the system is complex

A list or table of the key components in your system

A concise technological description of the system, process or product that you are working on (if this is not already present in your data room)

**Key technical parameters**:

Mass balance (mass flows in and out of your system)

Energy balance (electricity, heating, cooling, exo- or endothermicity of chemical reactions, etc.)

Operating conditions (temperature, pressure, voltage, current, flow rates, etc.)

Efficiencies

Yield

System capacity (equipment/reactor/plant sizing)

**Key financial parameters** to assess your unit economics, pricing and profitability (some of these might not be assessable until later stages of development, use reference data or proxies until you have your own data):

Capital expenditures (CAPEX): Building, machinery, reactors, equipment, balance of plant, installation, vehicles, IP, etc.

Operational expenditures (OPEX): Electricity, water, steam, chemicals, materials, salaries, rent, maintenance, insurance, etc.

Revenue projections

Profit/profit margins

Payback periods

Net present value (NPV)

Internal rate of return (IRR)

**Sensitivity analyses** to assess the impact of various different commercial and technical parameters. At the pre-seed stage you are likely to be focused on assessing and optimising technical performance parameters, like yield or energy efficiency.

A comparison of your technical and commercial KPIs with those of incumbents and other emerging technologies (**benchmarking**)

**Scaling analysis** (economies of scale + learning effects): Modelling your economics for a first-of-a-kind (FOAK) plant as well as the transition to an Nth-of-a-kind (NOAK) plant

An overview of potential **technical challenges** and **uncertainties** in your model

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_9d5ecee006eb444d88fddc742c83bd22~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_28,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_9d5ecee006eb444d88fddc742c83bd22~mv2.png)

*Factors contributing to the operational (OPEX) and capital expenditure (OPEX) of a company and therefore the unit economics.*

Remember: Like a good cuppa tea, your TEA needs to brew. **Don’t expect a perfect outcome (i.e. highly accurate predictions) the first time you build your TEA**. It’s an iterative process that requires time. Your accuracy will improve as you collect more and better data.

Check out the section below for more insights and recommendations on how to build a bullet-proof TEA.

## **What should I keep in mind?**

*Start early*: It is never too early to start building a TEA to identify the levers that are critical for your unit economics.

*Keep it digestible*: A TEA should be well-structured and transparent. Always remember that other people might not be experts in your domain and may therefore require additional guidance. Include an overview page that briefly describes the system, process or product that you are working on and explain your general approach to the TEA.

*Focus on what matters*: Start with the centrepiece of your system/process/product. Follow an 80/20 approach when working on your first versions and do not lose too much time trying to assess non-essential components. Simplify your calculations by making rational assumptions or using trustworthy reference data for supporting components/processes and balance of plant. These can be assessed at a later stage when more accurate data is available to you from testing or partnerships.

*Play with your variables*: Perform a sensitivity analysis (i.e. variation of one or more input variables) to test the assumptions you have made, and how variations affect the overall TEA. Which parameters are the most important?

*Use reliable data*: Any TEA is only as good as the data/assumptions used to build it. Be clear about your assumptions and provide references

*Constantly refine your TEA*: Remember that a TEA is a living document - use it to find your critical path and refine it regularly.

*Benchmark your startup*: Compare your unit economics to those of incumbents and competitors (including pre-commercial, emerging technologies).

*Think ahead*: Identify how you think main variables will change over time (e.g. with regulation, competition, economies of scale, new geographies/markets) and simulate different scenarios.

## **What are common pitfalls?**

At ZCC, we see a huge number of different TEAs every year. We have tried to put together a list of things that can lead to greater levels of uncertainty in your TEA (there is also an excellent Catalyst podcast that talks through some of these in more detail):

*Unreasonable assumptions*:

A very low power price (assuming 100% cheap renewables)

High capacity factors at very low power prices

Low feedstock prices (e.g. for green H2 and biogenic or captured CO2)

Unlimited feedstock availability (e.g. biomass)

Abundant waste heat availability

High green premiums for products with insufficient justification

Aggressive carbon pricing on incumbent technologies to justify own technologies

*Modelling the core technology instead of the full system*: The system boundary should be clearly defined. Take all equipment, engineering and balance of plant into consideration as these can add significant costs.

*Unrealistic or incomplete benchmarking*: It is important to not only benchmark against mature technologies but to also take emerging technologies into account. Compare your costs and sales price to the market prices of these technologies: What is the floor price of incumbents and newcomers? How high can your profit margin be?

*Focusing on the wrong or a limited number of metrics:* A good TEA takes all relevant metrics into consideration.

## Communicating your TEA

The main purpose of building a TEA is to help you understand your business, not simply to fulfil the due diligence requirements of an investor. That said, once built, it can be an extremely powerful tool to communicate with stakeholders: your board, your investors and potential hires. A well structured TEA can show how you think about your technology, your business, and the broader economy in which it sits. How best to communicate your TEA?

**Share the model itself**

investors want to be able to put in their own assumptions

they might be able to help your shape your TEA

**Diagrams!**

Process diagrams

Sensitivity analysis

Waterfall charts to show cost reductions

**Communicate your USP**

where does your technology shine?

## **What are reasonable assumptions for the prices of electricity, hydrogen and carbon dioxide?**

Given that many climate technologies depend heavily on electricity prices, and a significant number, on the cost of hydrogen, carbon dioxide or both, we thought it would be helpful to discuss the numbers here. Please note that all prices mentioned below are bound to change with time.

*Electricity costs:*

Your electricity costs will be heavily dependent on how you choose to source your electricity: will you be grid-connected, use a power purchase agreement (PPA) or be islanded and rely on solar-plus-storage? These are of course different scenarios you can model and determine your strategy accordingly. Where can you find the right values to input into your model?

Prices have shown large variability depending by region, and increased volatility over the last few years. You may need a to build different scenarios for each region, or take this into consideration for planning where to install early deployments.

Prices have shown large variability depending by region, and increased volatility

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_3fad0838b252462fab7abd48a430589b~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_27,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_3fad0838b252462fab7abd48a430589b~mv2.png)

*Quarterly average wholesale prices for selected regions in $/MWh (2019-2024). Source:*[*IEA*](https://www.iea.org/reports/electricity-market-report-update-2023/executive-summary)*.*

Our recommendation: €60-80/MWh (~$64-86/MWh) as a starting point

The [Lazard LCOE report](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/) gives values for different technologies (in $)

The lowest LCOE for solar/onshore wind is not available 24/7

Cost of energy available depends on ability to load-follow intermittency

Your technology may be able to load-follow intermittency to take advantage of the cheapest electricity prices, but this will impact capacity factors, and therefore OPEX

Use a sensitivity analysis to evaluate the impact of varying electricity prices

Model different scenarios for different energy procurement strategies

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_f9f0f43a74444e669ebecbb944980bb5~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_19,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_f9f0f43a74444e669ebecbb944980bb5~mv2.png)

*Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) for different renewable and conventional energy sources. Source:*[*Lazard 2024 LCOE+ Report*](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/)*.*

*Hydrogen costs:*

Electricity costs are the major component of hydrogen costs

Using a €60/MWh electricity cost and assuming energy requirements of 50 kWh/kg H2 (78% efficiency), electrolytic hydrogen production using the same electricity will cost at least €3.0/kg (~$3.2/kg)

There should be a sensible correlation between the electricity price assumed in your TEA and the green hydrogen price

The [Lazard LCOH report](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/) gives estimates of $2.5-6.1/kg for green hydrogen, depending on subsidies

Our recommendation: €3.5/kg (~$3.8/kg H2) as a conservative starting point

Use a sensitivity analysis to capture the uncertainty around future hydrogen prices

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_de8dd9f2b1ce44beb6687b61c2d435be~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_22,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_de8dd9f2b1ce44beb6687b61c2d435be~mv2.png)

*Levelised Cost of Hydrogen (LCOH) for green and pink hydrogen. Source:*[*Lazard 2024 LCOE+ Report*](https://www.lazard.com/research-insights/levelized-cost-of-energyplus/)*.*

*Carbon dioxide costs:*

Choosing a carbon dioxide price can be tricky due to the wide range of CDR technologies that are emerging

Costs for point-source captured CO2, biogenic CO2 and some types of biologically captured CO2 fall in the range of approximately €23-93/t CO2 ($25-100/t CO2)

Direct Air Capture (DAC) companies are aiming for €93/t CO2 ($100/t CO2, some even lower) but will inevitably need time to reach these prices

Use a CO2 price that is aligned with your CO2 suppliers/sources (refer to the IEA’s[report](https://www.iea.org/commentaries/is-carbon-capture-too-expensive) on carbon capture costs to get a better understanding for how CO2 costs can vary)

Use a sensitivity analysis to capture the uncertainty around future CO2 prices

![](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/39490a_23414af1d35c4f898f8c5336b17412f0~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_25,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/39490a_23414af1d35c4f898f8c5336b17412f0~mv2.png)

*Costs of capturing CO2 from different sources in $/t CO2. Source:*[*IEA*](https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/charts/levelised-cost-of-co2-capture-by-sector-and-initial-co2-concentration-2019)*.*

## **Where can I learn more about TEAs?**

Need more information or examples? Here is a list of resources that can guide you on your journey to your first TEA:

*What is a TEA?*

[Introduction to TEA](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UHuUtwyLChc) by the U.S. Department of Energy: Full series and PDF versions of training available[here](https://www.energy.gov/eere/iedo/life-cycle-assessment-and-techno-economic-analysis-training).

[Activate’s techno-economics section](https://www.activate.org/techonomics): Features models and videos that can help with early-stage cost modelling. Activate goes back to basics but offers good explanations.

[Catalyst podcast](https://www.canarymedia.com/podcasts/catalyst-with-shayle-kann/climatetech-startups-need-strong-techno-economic-analysis): Discusses TEA pitfalls.

*Templates:*

[Airminer’s templates](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/tea/templates-and-videos/)

[AssessCCUS website and templates](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/tea/templates-and-videos/): Various tips, tricks, links, videos and templates related to TEAs. Here are some of our favourite templates:[Aggregates](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Aggregates-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Algae](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Algae-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Chemicals](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Chemicals-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Concrete](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Concrete-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx), [Direct Air Capture](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/DAC-LCA-and-TEA-Template.xlsx).

[Planet A’s guide on TEA best practices](https://planet-a.medium.com/a-simple-founders-guide-to-teas-b469f423a2db) - you can access their TEA template[here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1dRZ8Sjv6lTFQgn7wKETxlFxzp-vixzHFj1xxubJorb4/edit?usp=sharing).

*Data sources & tools:*

[Conductor Labs](https://www.conductor-labs.com/) are building open-access tooling and data repositories for climate founders–watch this space. Learn more about Jesse Lou’s work in this [episode of the Climate Biotech podcast](https://open.spotify.com/episode/0D3M1B9cFb0PDe92ozDdRY).

[AssessCCUS Capital Equipment Cost Database](https://assessccus.globalco2initiative.org/wp-content/uploads/Capital-Equipment-Cost-Database.xlsx): The database contains estimated capital costs (CAPEX) for lots of different equipment. It is centered around CCUS equipment costs but is useful for hard tech in general.

[National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) website](https://www.nrel.gov/analysis/techno-economic.html): Features a Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) calculator, manufacturing analysis and solar technology cost analysis tools.

Did we miss something? Are you a founder or investor with an idea that will drastically improve every TEA? Then reach out to us! We would love to learn from you.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Techno-economic analysis provides stakeholders with essential data on cost, performance, and market potential, enabling informed decisions regarding technology investments and strategic direction.

TEA identifies economic feasibility and operational metrics, allowing innovators to streamline their commercialization strategies, prioritize resources, and navigate market entry challenges effectively.
By integrating technical and economic evaluations, TEA supports better decision-making, minimizing risks and maximizing returns through data-driven insights into project viability and scalability.
Techno-economic analysis evaluates environmental impacts alongside economic metrics, guiding the development of sustainable technologies that align with global energy transition goals.
